proposals.title: Re-evaluating Dietary Minimum-Risk Exposure: A Global Analysis of the Relationship Between Population-Level Nutrient Intake Distributions and Cardiovascular Disease Incidence
proposals.motivation: Current dietary guidelines often define minimum-risk exposure or 'optimal intake' based on the lowest disease rates observed within a single population or cohort. This approach assumes the population with the lowest incidence has achieved a biologically optimal intake. However, this may conflate optimal biology with the constraints of local food environments, genetics, and lifestyle, potentially misguiding global recommendations. A systematic, multi-population analysis is needed to test this foundational assumption.
proposals.hypothesis: The dietary intake associated with the lowest disease rate within a specific population does not represent a universal biological optimum but is instead a context-dependent point influenced by that population's overall dietary pattern and environmental exposures.
proposals.method: We will harmonize individual-level dietary data (from 24-hr recalls/records) and CVD event rates from large, international cohorts (e.g., PURE, EPIC, Framingham, Isfahan Healthy Heart Program). Using multivariable-adjusted Poisson or Cox regression within each cohort, we will model the non-linear relationship (via restricted cubic splines) between intake of key nutrients (e.g., PUFA, sodium, sugar) and CVD risk to identify cohort-specific nadirs. We will then employ meta-analytic techniques to pool these dose-response curves and formally test for heterogeneity in the nadir points across populations using the I² statistic and meta-regression on dietary context (e.g., overall diet quality score).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence on whether 'one-size-fits-all' intake targets are biologically justified or if recommendations should be contextualized within broader dietary patterns. It will advance methodological rigor in deriving dietary guidelines from observational data. The findings could shift policy focus from isolated nutrient targets to promoting overall healthy dietary patterns tailored to different food systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies associations within cohorts, this is the first to directly test the transposability of within-population minimum-risk points across diverse global populations. It moves beyond comparing mean intakes to analyzing the shape and nadir of dose-response curves as a function of dietary context.

proposals.title: Cost-Benefit Analysis and Value of Statistical Life Estimation for Prioritizing Dietary Interventions to Reduce Cardiovascular Disease Burden
proposals.motivation: Dietary risk factors are leading contributors to global cardiovascular disease (CVD) and mortality, yet public health resources are limited. Pre-2026, while evidence for interventions like the Mediterranean diet is strong, there is a critical gap in translating this evidence into actionable policy priorities using rigorous economic valuation. A systematic, data-driven economic framework is needed to compare the societal value of different dietary strategies, informing where investment yields the greatest health return.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying econometric methods to value the burden reduction from specific dietary changes will reveal a distinct, cost-effective priority order for interventions, with those targeting polyunsaturated fatty acid (PUFA) intake and salt reduction providing the highest societal value per capita.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cost-benefit analysis using a comparative risk assessment framework. First, we will model the population-attributable fraction for CVD events from changes in specific dietary factors (e.g., nuts, PUFAs, salt, sugar) using meta-analyses of cohort studies and randomized trials. Second, we will monetize the averted mortality and morbidity using established Value of Statistical Life (VSL) and cost-of-illness figures. Third, we will incorporate intervention costs from literature and model net benefits and benefit-cost ratios. Sensitivity analyses will test the robustness of rankings to VSL estimates and effect sizes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, directly comparable economic valuation of multiple evidence-based dietary interventions for CVD prevention. It will deliver a prioritized list for policymakers and payers, moving the field from efficacy to efficiency. The methodology will also establish a template for valuing dietary policies for other non-communicable diseases like cancer.
proposals.novelty: While cost-effectiveness analyses exist, applying full cost-benefit analysis with VSL to directly compare a suite of dietary factors is novel. Integrating econometric valuation with nutritional epidemiology to generate a monetized ranking specifically for dietary CVD prevention represents a significant methodological advance in the field.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Machine Learning Discovery of Dietary Patterns Associated with Cardiovascular Disease Risk in the Framingham Heart Study
proposals.motivation: Traditional dietary pattern analysis (e.g., Mediterranean diet) relies on a priori hypotheses, potentially missing complex, real-world dietary combinations that influence cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk. With the increasing availability of detailed, longitudinal food frequency data in large cohorts, there is a critical need for data-driven methods to identify novel, population-specific dietary patterns that may be protective or harmful, beyond established constructs like PUFA intake.
proposals.hypothesis: Unsupervised machine learning applied to high-dimensional dietary intake data will identify novel, data-driven dietary patterns that are significantly associated with incident CVD events, independent of traditional risk factors, and may reveal synergistic or antagonistic food combinations not captured by nutrient-level analyses.
proposals.method: We will apply unsupervised learning algorithms, specifically k-means clustering and Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs), to food group consumption data from the Framingham Heart Study Offspring cohort. Model selection will use the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). The derived dietary clusters will be validated for stability using bootstrapping. Subsequently, we will use Cox proportional hazards models to assess the association between cluster membership and incident CVD events (myocardial infarction, stroke, heart failure), adjusting for demographics, lifestyle, and clinical risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate novel, empirically-derived dietary patterns directly from population data, providing a more nuanced understanding of how foods are consumed in combination. It will test whether these data-driven patterns offer superior risk prediction compared to traditional diet scores and identify potential new targets for dietary interventions and public health messaging.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning is used for risk prediction, its application for purely unsupervised discovery of dietary patterns in established cardiovascular cohorts is limited. This approach moves beyond testing predefined patterns to letting the data reveal emergent structures, which is a significant methodological advance in nutritional epidemiology.

proposals.title: A Comprehensive Burden of Disease Analysis of Dietary Risk Factors: Expanding Beyond Cardiovascular Disease, Cancer, and Diabetes to Include Osteoporosis, Liver Disease, and Kidney Disease
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 Global Burden of Disease (GBD) studies and major policy analyses have primarily focused on the dietary contributions to cardiovascular disease, cancer, and type 2 diabetes. However, diet is a key modifiable risk factor for other major chronic conditions, including osteoporosis, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), and chronic kidney disease (CKD), which contribute substantially to global morbidity and healthcare costs. A comprehensive, integrated assessment is needed to inform holistic nutrition policy and clinical guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that expanding burden of disease calculations to include osteoporosis, NAFLD, and CKD will significantly increase the total estimated population-attributable fraction of disability and death due to suboptimal diets, and will reveal distinct priority nutrients (e.g., calcium, vitamin D, phosphorus, specific fatty acids) for preventing these additional conditions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a comparative risk assessment (CRA) analysis, extending methods used in prior GBD studies. We will systematically review and meta-analyze prospective cohort and randomized trial data (pre-2026) to define dose-response relationships between dietary factors (e.g., fruits, vegetables, nuts, whole grains, red/processed meat, sodium, sugar-sweetened beverages, calcium, vitamin D, PUFA intake) and incidence of osteoporosis-related fractures, NAFLD incidence/progression, and CKD incidence. Using nationally representative dietary survey data (e.g., NHANES), we will calculate theoretical minimum-risk exposure distributions and population-attributable fractions for each disease, then sum disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) across all six major diet-related diseases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified, quantitative estimate of the total global health burden attributable to diet across the six most consequential diet-related chronic diseases. It will generate disease-specific and nutrient-specific rankings to guide targeted dietary interventions. The findings will offer a robust evidence base for expanding the scope of national dietary guidelines and food policy initiatives beyond their traditional cardiometabolic focus.
proposals.novelty: While previous analyses have examined dietary risks for select diseases, this study integrates osteoporosis, NAFLD, and CKD into a single, comprehensive burden framework alongside the classic trio, using consistent CRA methodology to enable direct comparison of impact and identify overarching dietary priorities.

proposals.title: Inverse Diet-Disease Modeling: Quantifying Dietary Shifts Required to Achieve National Cardiovascular Disease Burden Reduction Targets in the United States
proposals.motivation: Current dietary guidelines are based on forward models linking foods to health outcomes. To meet concrete public health goals, such as the American Heart Association's 2030 target of a 20% reduction in cardiovascular disease (CVD) mortality, policymakers need to know the specific dietary changes required. This research directly addresses the gap between aspirational targets and actionable, quantitative dietary pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by inverting established diet-disease risk models, we can calculate a precise, prioritized set of dietary modifications (e.g., changes in nuts, seafood, processed meats, sugary beverages, and PUFA-rich oils) that, if adopted at the population level, would achieve predefined CVD burden reduction targets.
proposals.method: We will employ an inverse optimization framework. Using the US population as a case study, we will integrate pre-2026 meta-analytic dose-response relationships (e.g., from Mozaffarian's work on nuts, PUFA, and Mediterranean diet) for key dietary factors and CVD events into a consolidated risk model. The algorithm will treat the 20% CVD mortality reduction as a constraint and solve for the minimal set of dietary changes from current intake levels (assessed via NHANES) needed to satisfy this constraint. Sensitivity analyses will rank factors by impact and feasibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, target-driven 'dietary prescription' for a major US public health goal, moving beyond generic advice. It will offer policymakers and nutritionists a prioritized intervention roadmap and establish a novel methodological framework applicable to other diseases and nations.
proposals.novelty: While forward diet-disease models are common, inverting them to solve for required intake changes given a health outcome target is a novel, engineering-inspired approach to nutritional epidemiology. It shifts the paradigm from 'what might happen if we change diet?' to 'what must we change to reach our goal?'

proposals.title: Modeling the Economic Determinants of Global Dietary Risk: A Causal Analysis of Food Prices, Trade Policy, and Cardiovascular Disease Burden
proposals.motivation: Global dietary patterns, characterized by high intakes of salt, sugar, and unhealthy fats, are the leading cause of cardiovascular disease (CVD) mortality. While individual dietary choices are often studied, the upstream economic drivers—including food prices, agricultural subsidies, and international trade agreements—that shape the food environment remain poorly quantified. Understanding these systemic drivers is critical for developing effective, population-level policy interventions to improve nutrition and reduce CVD burden.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that national and regional variations in the relative prices of healthy versus unhealthy food commodities, driven by agricultural and trade policies, causally explain a significant portion of the global distribution of dietary risk factors (e.g., low PUFA intake, high sodium intake) and, consequently, population-level CVD event rates.
proposals.method: We will construct a longitudinal global dataset (2000-2025) linking FAO food balance sheets, World Bank price indices, OECD agricultural support estimates, and trade data with dietary exposure estimates from the Global Dietary Database and CVD mortality from the Global Burden of Disease study. Using a structural equation modeling (SEM) framework with instrumental variables (e.g., climate shocks for crop-specific prices), we will estimate the causal pathways from policy levers to food prices, from prices to dietary intake, and from intake to disease burden. Country and year fixed effects will control for unobserved confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first globally comprehensive, causal model quantifying how specific economic policies translate into dietary risk and CVD outcomes. It will move the field beyond descriptive associations to identify actionable economic leverage points for policy change. The findings will directly inform recommendations for aligning agricultural and trade policies with public health goals, a gap highlighted in recent global nutrition frameworks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described dietary patterns or linked single policies to outcomes, this analysis integrates multiple economic drivers into a unified causal model to explain global dietary exposure distributions, directly bridging agricultural economics and nutritional epidemiology in a novel way.

proposals.title: Non-Linear Dose-Response Relationships of Industrial Trans Fatty Acid Intake with Cardiovascular Events and All-Cause Mortality: A Pooled Analysis of Prospective Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, extensive evidence from meta-analyses and prospective cohort studies has established a strong, positive linear association between industrial trans fatty acid (iTFA) intake and increased risk of cardiovascular disease (CVD) and mortality. However, this linear assumption, which underpins public health guidelines advocating for 'as low as reasonably achievable' intake, has rarely been rigorously tested for potential non-linear or threshold effects. Determining the precise shape of the dose-response curve is critical for refining dietary guidance and understanding whether there is a safe lower threshold of intake or if risk accelerates at higher doses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationship between iTFA intake and clinical endpoints (major adverse cardiovascular events and all-cause mortality) is non-linear, characterized by a steeper increase in risk at lower levels of intake (e.g., <2% of energy) that plateaus at higher intakes, or exhibits a distinct risk threshold below which the association is markedly attenuated.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pooled analysis of individual-level data from large, well-characterized prospective cohorts with validated dietary assessments (e.g., repeated food frequency questionnaires) and long-term follow-up for clinical events. Using pre-specified, restricted cubic spline models within a multivariable-adjusted Cox proportional hazards framework, we will formally test for non-linearity. We will perform sensitivity analyses using fractional polynomial models and piecewise linear regression to identify potential inflection points. Subgroup analyses will assess consistency across populations and by food sources of iTFA.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the most detailed characterization to date of the iTFA dose-response curve, moving beyond a simple linear coefficient. It will directly inform global dietary policy by quantifying risk gradients at very low levels of consumption. The findings could either strengthen the case for complete elimination of iTFA from the food supply or identify a potential tolerance level, with significant implications for food technology and regulatory standards.
proposals.novelty: While the harms of iTFA are well-established, the field has largely assumed a linear relationship without formal testing. This study applies advanced, pre-specified non-linear modeling techniques—common in environmental toxicology but underutilized in nutritional epidemiology for iTFA—to directly challenge this foundational assumption and explore nuanced risk patterns.

proposals.title: A Participatory Systems Modeling Framework to Identify Feasible Trans-Fat Reduction Pathways in Low- and Middle-Income Countries
proposals.motivation: Industrially produced trans-fatty acids (TFA) are a major, preventable cause of cardiovascular disease mortality globally. While the WHO has called for global elimination by 2023, low-resource food systems face unique economic, technical, and cultural barriers that hinder the adoption of blanket policies like outright bans. A one-size-fits-all approach risks economic disruption and low compliance, failing to achieve public health goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a context-specific, participatory decision framework, which systematically diagnoses local food system barriers and co-designs staged intervention portfolios, will identify more feasible and effective TFA reduction pathways for diverse low-resource settings than generic policy recommendations.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods, systems-oriented approach. First, we will conduct systematic barrier diagnosis using stakeholder interviews, market surveys, and food composition analysis in 2-3 case countries. Second, we will develop an agent-based model (ABM) representing key actors (producers, regulators, consumers) and their interactions. Using participatory system dynamics workshops, we will parameterize the ABM to simulate and evaluate portfolios of interventions (e.g., phased bans, reformulation subsidies, PUFA oil promotion). The model will output optimized, staged implementation sequences based on cost, feasibility, and projected health impact metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, generalizable typology of food system archetypes and their corresponding feasible TFA reduction pathways. It will provide policymakers in LMICs with a practical tool for moving from barrier assessment to actionable, staged strategy design, directly supporting the WHO's REPLACE framework with implementation science.
proposals.novelty: This integrates participatory system dynamics with agent-based modeling for food policy design, moving beyond descriptive barrier analyses to simulate the dynamic outcomes of intervention combinations in specific contexts, which is not addressed in existing global TFA research.

proposals.title: Triangulating the Causal Effects of Industrially-Produced Trans Fatty Acids on Visceral Adiposity and Insulin Resistance: A Multi-Method Cohort and Policy Analysis
proposals.motivation: Despite bans in some regions, industrially-produced trans fatty acids (IP-TFAs) remain a global dietary risk factor. Pre-2026 evidence strongly links IP-TFAs to coronary heart disease, but their specific causal effects on visceral adiposity and insulin resistance—key drivers of cardiometabolic disease—are less definitively established. Ethical constraints preclude a randomized trial, creating a need for robust, alternative causal inference methods to inform ongoing global policy decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher exposure to IP-TFAs causes a disproportionate increase in visceral adipose tissue and worsens insulin resistance, independent of overall adiposity, and that population-level TFA restrictions will lead to measurable improvements in these metabolic parameters.
proposals.method: We will employ a triangulation framework. First, we will analyze serial biomarker (e.g., erythrocyte membrane TFA content) and imaging (MRI/CT) data from existing cohorts (e.g., MESA, Framingham) to model dose-response relationships. Second, we will use difference-in-differences and interrupted time-series analyses to compare metabolic outcomes before and after TFA policy implementation in jurisdictions like the US (2015-2018 FDA removal of GRAS status) versus matched control regions. Third, we will conduct a two-sample Mendelian randomization study using genetic variants associated with TFA metabolism (e.g., FADS1/2) as instrumental variables to infer causality.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the most robust, multi-angled causal evidence to date on the specific metabolic harms of IP-TFAs, directly linking them to visceral fat and insulin resistance. It will generate actionable evidence for policymakers in regions without bans and offer a novel analytical framework for studying other harmful nutrients where RCTs are unethical.
proposals.novelty: This integrates deep phenotyping with objective TFA biomarkers, natural policy experiments, and genetic epidemiology into a single triangulation design—a novel approach in nutritional epidemiology that moves beyond observational associations to robust causal inference for a well-established harmful exposure.

proposals.title: A Systems Dynamics Model of the Global Trans-Fatty Acid Policy-Trade Nexus to Optimize Coordinated Elimination Strategies
proposals.motivation: Despite WHO's REPLACE initiative to eliminate industrial trans-fatty acids (TFA) by 2023, progress is uneven, with many low- and middle-income countries lacking regulations. Pre-2026 evidence shows that unilateral policies in high-income countries can displace partially hydrogenated oils (PHOs) and TFA-rich products into less-regulated markets via global trade, potentially worsening health inequities. A systems-level analysis is urgently needed to understand these feedback loops and design strategies that maximize global, not just national, cardiovascular disease prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: Coordinated, multi-faceted policy packages (e.g., combining bans with trade agreements and support for healthier fat alternatives) will lead to faster and more equitable global TFA elimination and greater reductions in projected CVD events than isolated national policies, by proactively managing economic and trade displacements.
proposals.method: We will construct a global systems dynamics model integrating data from FAOSTAT (trade flows), GLOBOCAN (disease burden), and WHO policy databases. The model will represent key stocks (e.g., PHO inventories, alternative fat production capacity) and flows (trade, consumption) across major regional nodes. Policy levers (bans, labeling, subsidies for PUFA-rich oils) will be parameterized using pre-2026 efficacy estimates. Simulation and optimization algorithms, including Monte Carlo methods for uncertainty and gradient-based search for optimal policy sequences, will be used to identify intervention mixes that minimize global attributable DALYs over a 20-year horizon.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative tool to simulate the international spillover effects of TFA policies, moving beyond single-country evaluations. It will provide evidence-based, prioritized policy packages for global health agencies and national governments, directly supporting the WHO's goal. The model framework will also be adaptable for other nutrition-related trade challenges, such as those involving sugar or salt.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has modeled dietary risks or policy cost-effectiveness in isolation, this integrates detailed global trade economics with food policy and disease burden models to capture critical cross-border feedback, a gap in pre-2026 public health nutrition research.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Public Health Impact: A Comparative Microsimulation Model of Coronary Heart Disease Events Prevented by Trans-Fatty Acid (TFA) Legislative Policies in the United States
proposals.motivation: Industrial trans-fatty acids (TFA) are a well-established, modifiable dietary risk factor for coronary heart disease (CHD). While policies to restrict or eliminate TFAs have been implemented in various jurisdictions, there is a critical need for a detailed, comparative assessment of their long-term public health impact. Grounded in pre-2026 evidence, this research will provide policymakers with robust, actionable estimates to inform future food policy decisions aimed at reducing cardiovascular disease burden.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a comprehensive national ban on partially hydrogenated oils (PHOs), the primary dietary source of industrial TFA, will prevent a substantially greater number of CHD events over a 20-year period compared to weaker policies, such as mandatory labeling or local bans, and that the benefits will be greatest among socioeconomically disadvantaged populations.
proposals.method: We will develop a discrete-time microsimulation model using a US-representative synthetic population from NHANES data. The model will incorporate pre-2026 risk equations (e.g., from the Framingham Heart Study or the Pooled Cohort Equations) to project individual CHD risk. Policy effects will be modeled as changes in TFA intake distributions, informed by pre- and post-policy intake data from regions with existing TFA policies (e.g., New York City, Denmark). We will compare scenarios: 1) Status quo (pre-2015 labeling), 2) National PHO ban (FDA 2015 determination), 3) Local bans only, and 4) Mandatory labeling only. The model will be validated against observed CHD trends where possible.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comparative, policy-specific estimates of CHD events prevented by different TFA legislative approaches in the US. It will directly quantify the long-term health and economic value of the FDA's PHO ban and offer a transparent modeling framework for evaluating other dietary policies, such as those targeting sugar or sodium.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have estimated TFA-related mortality, this proposal innovates by modeling the comparative effectiveness of specific, real-world policy levers (ban vs. label) on CHD event incidence, and by explicitly evaluating equity impacts through stratified analysis by socioeconomic status.

proposals.title: Network Analysis of the Industrial Trans Fat Supply Chain and Simulation of a Dynamic Intervention Model for Cardiovascular Risk Reduction
proposals.motivation: Despite strong evidence linking industrial trans fatty acid (TFA) intake to increased cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk, population-level mitigation remains a complex challenge involving food manufacturing, distribution, and policy. Pre-2026, interventions have often been static, lacking a feedback loop between supply-chain modifications and real-world health outcomes. Integrating systems science with chronic disease epidemiology could create more agile, data-driven public health strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Mapping the industrial TFA supply network will reveal critical, high-leverage control points (e.g., specific manufacturers, distributors, or product categories) where targeted interventions can maximize population-level CVD risk reduction, as measured through simulated changes in dietary intake and projected clinical events.
proposals.method: We will first construct a multi-layer network model of the U.S. industrial TFA supply chain using pre-2026 datasets (e.g., Nielsen retail scanner data, industry reports, ingredient databases) to identify nodes and flows. We will apply network centrality algorithms (e.g., betweenness, eigenvector centrality) to pinpoint high-leverage control points. Subsequently, we will integrate this network model with a validated CVD risk simulation model (e.g., based on Framingham risk equations or a microsimulation) to create a feedback loop, iteratively assessing the projected impact of hypothetical supply-chain interventions (e.g., product reformulation at key nodes) on population dietary TFA intake and incident CVD events over a 10-year horizon.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, systems-oriented framework for public health nutrition, moving beyond traditional dietary advice to operationalize supply-chain cardiology. It will deliver a prioritized list of actionable supply-chain intervention targets and a simulation platform to estimate their potential cardiovascular benefits, directly informing policymakers and industry stakeholders.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its explicit fusion of supply-chain network analysis from operations research with chronic disease care modeling from cardiology, creating a dynamic, feedback-driven system for proactive food environment risk management, rather than retrospective trace-back or passive monitoring.

proposals.title: Targeting Mitochondrial and Endoplasmic Reticulum Stress with a Precision Mediterranean Diet Intervention to Improve Metabolic Health in Cancer Survivors: The PREVENT Trial
proposals.motivation: Cancer survivors face elevated risks of recurrence, second cancers, and cardiometabolic disease, with chronic inflammation and metabolic dysfunction as key drivers. Pre-2026 evidence links diets high in trans fats and low in omega-3 PUFAs to systemic inflammation and organelle stress, but the application of this mechanistic knowledge to structured, biomarker-guided lifestyle interventions for survivorship care remains unexplored. This gap represents a critical opportunity to move beyond generic dietary advice to biologically-personalized strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a precision Mediterranean diet intervention, tailored to reduce intake of pro-inflammatory lipids (e.g., trans fats) and increase protective nutrients (e.g., marine omega-3 PUFAs, polyphenols), will significantly improve biomarkers of mitochondrial function and endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress in immune cells, leading to reductions in systemic inflammation and insulin resistance among cancer survivors compared to standard survivorship care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month randomized controlled trial in 150 adult survivors of common cancers (e.g., breast, colorectal). The intervention arm will receive a personalized Mediterranean diet plan, with prescriptions dynamically adjusted every 3 months based on a panel of blood-based biomarkers, including circulating cell-free mitochondrial DNA (cf-mtDNA, a marker of mitochondrial stress), plasma GDF-15, and the ER stress marker CHOP. Dietary intake will be assessed via repeated 24-hour recalls and blood fatty acid profiles. We will use linear mixed-effects models to analyze changes in primary outcomes (HOMA-IR, hs-CRP) and the organelle stress biomarkers, testing for mediation effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel framework for 'precision nutrition' in oncology survivorship by directly linking dietary prescriptions to quantifiable improvements in subcellular organelle health. It will generate the first clinical evidence on whether modulating organelle stress through diet can ameliorate treatment-related metabolic sequelae. The findings could redefine dietary guidelines for cancer survivors from population-level recommendations to a dynamic, biomarker-monitored model.
proposals.novelty: While the cardiometabolic benefits of Mediterranean diets are known, their application as a dynamically-adjusted, organelle-targeted therapy for cancer survivors is novel. The integration of cf-mtDNA and ER stress biomarkers as actionable feedback tools for dietary personalization represents a significant advance beyond static dietary counseling.

proposals.title: Phylodynamic Modeling of Global Trans-Fatty Acid Trade as an Epidemic Network for Cardiovascular Risk
proposals.motivation: Despite global efforts to eliminate industrial trans-fatty acids (iTFA), their persistence in the global food supply via international trade remains a major, unquantified driver of cardiovascular disease (CVD). Current dietary risk models treat national intakes as static, failing to capture the dynamic flow of iTFA from producer 'reservoirs' through trade networks to consumer populations. This gap limits our ability to attribute CVD burden to specific trade flows and to evaluate the effectiveness of national iTFA policies in an interconnected global market.
proposals.hypothesis: The global trade network for partially hydrogenated oils (PHOs) and iTFA-rich foods functions as an epidemic transmission system, where specific export 'source' countries act as persistent reservoirs, and the temporal 'flow' of this dietary risk factor predicts downstream population-level changes in CVD biomarkers and events with a measurable lag.
proposals.method: We will adapt phylodynamic source-sink models and network analysis from infectious disease epidemiology. Using UN Comtrade and FAO food balance sheet data (2000-2025), we will construct a time-varying, directed network of PHO and key commodity flows. Country-specific iTFA compositional 'signatures' will be inferred from historical regulatory status, fat composition databases, and branded food data. We will apply the structured coalescent framework (e.g., in BEAST) to this network to identify major source hubs and transmission routes. These estimated iTFA 'import exposure' time-series will be integrated with lagged correlations in country-level health data (e.g., plasma phospholipid TFA, LDL cholesterol, and coronary heart disease mortality from the Global Burden of Disease study) using mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dynamic, network-based model of global dietary risk transmission, moving beyond static intake estimates. It will generate novel metrics of 'effective iTFA export' and 'population exposure via trade,' offering a proof-of-concept for tracking other dietary risks (e.g., sugar, salt) through global markets. The findings will inform international food policy by identifying critical intervention points within the trade network to accelerate global iTFA elimination.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its conceptual adaptation of pathogen phylodynamics—a method used to trace viral spread through travel networks—to model the flow of a dietary carcinogen/cardiovascular risk factor through trade data, treating commodity composition as a heritable 'trait' for transmission inference.

proposals.title: Global Trade Flows of Partially Hydrogenated Oils and Associations with National Cardiovascular Disease Mortality: A Novel Ecological Analysis
proposals.motivation: Industrial trans-fatty acids (TFA) from partially hydrogenated oils (PHOs) are a leading dietary cause of cardiovascular disease (CVD). While many high-income countries have enacted TFA bans, global trade data suggest PHOs may be exported to and consumed in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) with less stringent regulations. Pre-2026, there is a critical gap in understanding how international commodity trade directly influences population-level dietary risks for CVD.
proposals.hypothesis: Nations that are net importers of PHOs and related commodities will have higher population-level CVD mortality rates, independent of other dietary and socioeconomic factors, and this burden will disproportionately affect LMICs.
proposals.method: We will obtain annual, country-level data on trade of PHOs and major source oils (e.g., soybean, palm) from UN Comtrade and FAO databases (2000-2020). We will calculate net import metrics and estimate per capita TFA availability. These will be linked with age-standardized CVD mortality rates from the Global Burden of Disease study. Analyses will employ fixed-effects panel regression models, adjusting for GDP, urbanization, and other dietary covariates (sugar, salt availability). We will use directed acyclic graphs to inform model specification and assess lagged effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical, longitudinal evidence linking global food trade patterns directly to a major diet-related disease burden. It will identify specific countries and trade flows representing priority targets for policy intervention. The findings will directly inform global surveillance and advocacy efforts, such as the WHO REPLACE initiative, by quantifying the health impact of unregulated TFA trade.
proposals.novelty: Prior ecological studies on TFA and health rely on estimated dietary intake; this is the first to use objective international trade data as a proxy for population exposure. The approach moves beyond national policies to examine the globalized food system as a direct determinant of CVD risk.

proposals.title: Personalized Biomarker Feedback for Dietary Motivation in Cancer Survivors: A Randomized Trial of Historical Trans Fat Exposure Quantification
proposals.motivation: Cancer survivors face elevated risks of cardiovascular disease and other chronic conditions, making dietary modification critical. The 'teachable moment' of a cancer diagnosis is underutilized, and generic dietary advice often fails to motivate sustained change, particularly across diverse demographic and psychological subgroups. Industrial trans fatty acids (TFA) are a well-established, modifiable dietary risk factor with stable biomarkers that reflect long-term intake, offering a unique opportunity for objective, personalized risk communication.
proposals.hypothesis: Providing cancer survivors with a biomarker-quantified, personalized estimate of their historical industrial TFA exposure will be more effective than generic dietary advice in motivating sustained, healthful dietary change, and this effect will be consistent across demographic and psychological subgroups where self-reported measures often show disparities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized controlled trial in 500 adult cancer survivors (1-5 years post-diagnosis). The intervention arm will receive feedback based on their adipose tissue or erythrocyte membrane TFA content, analyzed via gas chromatography, translated into an estimated 'TFA-years' exposure metric using a validated pharmacokinetic model (inverse problem approach). The control arm will receive standard, evidence-based dietary guidance. Primary outcome is change in a validated dietary score (e.g., AHEI-2010) at 6 and 12 months, assessed via repeated 24-hour recalls. We will use mixed-effects models to test for group differences and moderation by sociodemographic and psychological factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence on whether biomarker-based personalization enhances the motivational power of a major life event for chronic disease prevention. It will advance methods for translating stable biological signatures into actionable health feedback. The findings will inform precision nutrition strategies for secondary prevention in a high-risk population.
proposals.novelty: It uniquely fuses the concept of a 'teachable moment' with objective, retrospective exposure biomarker quantification to personalize prevention, moving beyond generic advice or forward-looking biomarkers. The study directly tests if this biological grounding can overcome known barriers to behavior change that limit self-report-based interventions.

proposals.title: Dietary Countermeasures to Trans Fat-Induced Lipotoxicity: A Nutrigenomic Investigation of Organelle-Specific Rescue in Cardiometabolic Cell Models
proposals.motivation: Despite regulatory action, industrially produced trans fatty acids (iTFA) remain a global cardiovascular and metabolic disease risk factor. Pre-2026 evidence indicates iTFA disrupt mitochondrial function and ER homeostasis, but the capacity of specific dietary bioactives to counteract this organelle-level damage is poorly defined. This mechanistic gap limits the development of targeted, evidence-based nutritional strategies to mitigate the residual and historical harms of iTFA consumption.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific dietary bioactive compounds (e.g., n-3 PUFAs, polyphenols like resveratrol or curcumin, and taurine) will prevent or attenuate iTFA-induced dysfunction in a pathway-specific manner, with n-3 PUFAs primarily restoring mitochondrial membrane integrity and bioenergetics, while polyphenols and taurine preferentially mitigate ER stress and unfolded protein response signaling.
proposals.method: We will use in vitro and ex vivo models of human hepatocytes (HepG2), adipocytes, and induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes. Cells will be exposed to elaidic acid (C18:1 trans-9) with or without pre-/co-treatment with candidate bioactives. Outcomes will be quantified via Seahorse XF Analyzer for mitochondrial respiration, fluorescent probes (e.g., JC-1, MitoSOX) for membrane potential and ROS, and immunoblotting for ER stress markers (PERK, IRE1α, CHOP, XBP1 splicing). A factorial design will test for additive or synergistic effects of bioactive combinations.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a high-resolution, mechanistic map linking specific dietary countermeasures to the precise organelle pathways they rescue from iTFA insult. It will provide foundational experimental evidence for a precision nutrition framework aimed at lipotoxicity, moving beyond population-level dietary patterns to targeted, bioactive-based interventions.
proposals.novelty: While the harms of trans fats and benefits of various nutrients are known, this study uniquely positions specific dietary bioactives as direct molecular antidotes to defined organelle dysfunctions, creating a predictive 'damage-countermeasure' matrix rather than observing general associations.

proposals.title: Target Trial Emulation of a Mediterranean-Style Lifestyle Program on Cardiovascular Mortality in Post-Myocardial Infarction Survivors: An Analysis of the REGARDS and MESA Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Secondary prevention after a major cardiovascular event is critical, yet the real-world, long-term mortality benefit of initiating structured lifestyle programs remains uncertain due to confounding in observational studies. While trials like PREDIMED show primary prevention benefits, a pragmatic randomized trial in secondary prevention is logistically challenging. Emulating such a trial using existing cohort data can provide robust, near-causal evidence to guide post-event care.
proposals.hypothesis: Initiating a structured, Mediterranean-style lifestyle improvement program within one year of a myocardial infarction causally reduces the risk of cardiovascular mortality over 10-year follow-up, compared to usual care.
proposals.method: We will emulate a target trial using pooled data from REGARDS and MESA. The protocol will define eligibility (adults with incident MI), treatment strategies (program initiation vs. non-initiation, based on dietary and physical activity surveys), and outcome (CV mortality). We will use cloning, censoring, and weighting to address immortal time bias and confounding. Specifically, we will apply sequential target trial emulation with time-varying inverse probability of treatment and censoring weights (IPTW & IPCWs) to estimate a marginal structural model for the hazard ratio.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first causal estimates of a multi-component lifestyle intervention on hard mortality endpoints in secondary prevention, moving beyond association. It will demonstrate the practical application of target trial emulation to nutrition and lifestyle epidemiology, offering a rigorous analytical template for evaluating real-world program effectiveness.
proposals.novelty: This applies the cutting-edge target trial emulation framework, recently advanced by Hernán and Robins, specifically to the question of structured lifestyle intervention in a high-risk survivor population, a context where traditional RCTs are scarce. It integrates detailed, time-updated dietary data (e.g., Mediterranean diet score, PUFA intake) with clinical outcomes to model a complex behavioral intervention.

proposals.title: Causal Mediation Analysis of a Multi-Factorial Lifestyle Intervention on Cardiovascular Mortality in High-Risk Survivors: A Randomized Controlled Trial Applying a Systems Biology Framework
proposals.motivation: While observational studies strongly link healthy diets and lifestyles to lower cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk, definitive proof that lifestyle interventions directly reduce mortality in survivors remains elusive. This mirrors the challenge in ecological restoration, where proving an intervention's effect on a final endpoint (e.g., species survival) requires mapping causal pathways through intermediate system states. A mechanistic understanding is critical to optimize public health and clinical guidelines for secondary prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, multi-factorial lifestyle intervention directly reduces cardiovascular mortality in high-risk survivors, and this effect is mediated through quantifiable improvements in a defined network of intermediate physiological and biochemical pathways, including vascular endothelial function, systemic inflammation, and cardiac autonomic regulation.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, randomized controlled trial with long-term follow-up (minimum 5 years) enrolling adults with prior major CVD events. The intervention arm receives an intensive, Mediterranean-style dietary program (emphasizing nuts, PUFA-rich oils, fruits, vegetables), physical activity, and stress management, modeled on successful pre-2026 trials. The primary outcome is cardiovascular mortality. Crucially, we will employ a pre-specified causal mediation analysis framework. We will serially measure a suite of mechanistic biomarkers (e.g., flow-mediated dilation, hs-CRP, IL-6, heart rate variability, plasma omega-3 fatty acid levels) as potential mediators. Using structural equation modeling and counterfactual-based mediation analysis, we will quantify the proportion of the total treatment effect on mortality explained by changes in these interconnected pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the highest level of evidence for a causal link between lifestyle change and reduced cardiovascular mortality, moving beyond association. It will identify the key physiological pathways through which lifestyle exerts its benefit, allowing for more targeted and personalized secondary prevention strategies. The analytical framework will set a new standard for demonstrating causality in lifestyle and nutritional epidemiology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly adapting the causal pathway mapping approach from ecological restoration to human clinical trials and employing formal, pre-specified causal mediation analysis with a comprehensive panel of mechanistic biomarkers to deconstruct the 'black box' of lifestyle intervention effects.

proposals.title: A Community-Led Intervention Using Trade Data Surveillance to Reduce Local Supply of Industrially Produced Trans-Fatty Acids: A Feasibility and Impact Study
proposals.motivation: Despite clear evidence of harm, industrially produced trans-fatty acids (IP-TFAs) remain prevalent in global food supplies, particularly in low- and middle-income countries, due to the trade of partially hydrogenated oils. Pre-2026 public health strategies often focus on national regulation or consumer education, but these can be slow and may not address local market realities where harmful products continue to flow. Empowering communities with precise supply-chain data could create a novel, bottom-up pressure point for dietary risk reduction.
proposals.hypothesis: Equipping community health groups with analyzed international trade data on IP-TFA-containing oils will enable them to effectively identify and target local importers and retailers, leading to a measurable reduction in the availability of these products and an associated decrease in population-level TFA intake.
proposals.method: We will partner with community health groups in two urban districts. Using pre-2026 UN Comtrade and national customs data, we will develop a surveillance algorithm to map the flow of specific HS-coded edible oils and fats from exporting countries to local importers. This data, visualized via GIS mapping, will be provided to community groups who will design and implement targeted advocacy (e.g., engaging importers), consumer awareness campaigns, and promote alternative procurement. Pre- and post-intervention, we will audit retail outlets for IP-TFA content (using label surveillance and targeted GC-MS testing) and measure population TFA intake via blood phospholipid fatty acid profiles in a cohort.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a proof-of-concept for a novel, data-driven community empowerment model to improve food environments. It will generate evidence on the feasibility and impact of local, supply-chain-focused interventions as a complement to national policy. The findings could inform new participatory public health strategies for reducing dietary risks like TFA, sugar, and salt.
proposals.novelty: It shifts the primary intervention agency from clinical/public health entities to community groups using trade intelligence as a strategic tool, transforming community action from education to targeted economic disruption. The fusion of granular international trade data with hyper-local advocacy and biomarker validation is a novel methodological approach.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Smartphone-Delivered Digital Nudge Intervention to Replace Industrial Trans Fatty Acids with Healthier Fats and Its Impact on Blood Lipid Profiles
proposals.motivation: Despite the FDA's 2015 determination that partially hydrogenated oils are no longer 'Generally Recognized as Safe,' industrial trans fats (iTFA) persist in the food supply, contributing to cardiovascular disease risk. Scalable, effective strategies are needed to help consumers identify and replace these harmful fats. This study directly addresses this gap by testing a digital behavioral intervention with a precise biochemical outcome, linking dietary change to cardiometabolic health.
proposals.hypothesis: A personalized, theory-based digital nudge intervention delivered via smartphone will significantly reduce the ratio of industrial to natural (ruminant) trans fatty acids in blood plasma and improve associated cardiometabolic biomarkers (e.g., LDL-C, HDL-C, triglycerides) compared to a control condition.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 6-month, parallel-group RCT with 300 adults at elevated CVD risk. The intervention group will use a custom app employing algorithms for personalized feedback, including: 1) a barcode scanner linked to a pre-2026 branded food database to flag iTFA-containing products, 2) a recommendation engine suggesting swaps to foods with healthier fats (e.g., nuts, seeds, oils high in PUFA), and 3) gamified challenges based on behavioral economic principles (e.g., loss aversion, commitment devices). The control app will provide general healthy eating education. The primary outcome is the change in the plasma phospholipid ratio of elaidic acid (iTFA biomarker) to vaccenic acid (ruminant TFA biomarker), measured via gas chromatography.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide high-level evidence on the efficacy of a scalable digital tool to modify a specific, hard-to-change dietary risk factor. It will directly bridge the fields of behavioral economics and nutritional biochemistry by quantifying how digitally nudged behavior changes translate into objective, cardiometabolic biomarker shifts, offering a model for future precision nutrition interventions.
proposals.novelty: While digital dietary interventions exist, none have specifically targeted the replacement of industrial trans fats with precision using a biomarker-validated outcome. The fusion of a nudge-based behavioral architecture with the biochemical specificity of distinguishing industrial from natural trans fat isomers is a novel and methodologically rigorous approach.

proposals.title: Leveraging Non-Cardiovascular Hospitalizations as a Teachable Moment for Population-Wide Dietary Intervention: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Cardiovascular disease (CVD) remains the leading cause of death globally. While 'teachable moments' like a heart attack are effective for secondary prevention, they miss the larger population at risk. Scaling primary prevention requires identifying and intervening at other high-leverage, common life events that increase receptivity to lifestyle change. Non-cardiovascular hospitalizations represent a major, underutilized opportunity for population-wide CVD prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: A scalable, hospital-initiated dietary intervention (focused on increasing PUFA, nuts, and Mediterranean-style patterns) delivered during the 'teachable moment' of a non-cardiovascular hospitalization will lead to sustained, clinically meaningful improvements in dietary quality and reduce composite CVD risk factors in a general population cohort, compared to usual care.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, pragmatic randomized controlled trial. Adults hospitalized for non-cardiovascular causes (e.g., elective surgery, infections) will be randomized to intervention or usual care. The intervention will use a validated, automated electronic health record alert to identify eligible patients and trigger a consultation with a health coach. The coach will deliver a structured, patient-centered dietary program using motivational interviewing and provide 6 months of tailored support via a mobile health platform. Primary outcomes are change in a validated dietary risk score (e.g., using food frequency questionnaires) and a composite of blood pressure, lipids, and inflammatory biomarkers at 12 months.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-level evidence for systematically using non-cardiac hospitalizations as a scalable platform for primary CVD prevention. It will generate a practical, protocolized intervention model that can be integrated into standard hospital workflow. The findings could shift public health strategy by repurposing routine healthcare contacts into powerful prevention opportunities.
proposals.novelty: While teachable moments are recognized in cardiac rehab, this systematically applies the concept to the vastly larger pool of non-cardiac hospitalizations for primary prevention. It moves beyond studying single nutrients or foods to test a scalable systems-level intervention that leverages existing healthcare infrastructure and digital tools for population impact.

proposals.title: Differential Effects of Raw, Salted, and Sugar-Coated Nut Consumption on Cardiometabolic Risk Factors: A Prospective Cohort Analysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence robustly links nut consumption to cardiovascular benefits, but this is based largely on studies of raw or unsalted nuts. In real-world diets, a significant portion of nut intake comes from processed, salted, or sugar-coated varieties. The health impact of these common, commercially available forms is unknown, creating a critical evidence gap for dietary guidelines and consumer choices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while consumption of raw nuts will be associated with improved cardiometabolic risk profiles, frequent intake of salted nuts will be associated with higher blood pressure, and frequent intake of sugar-coated nuts will be associated with adverse glycemic and lipid profiles, attenuating or reversing the benefits seen with raw nuts.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective analysis using existing cohort data (e.g., NHS, HPFS, or MESA) with detailed, validated food-frequency questionnaires that distinguish nut types. Primary exposures will be frequency and quantity of raw, salted, and sugar-coated nut consumption. Primary outcomes will be incident hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and composite cardiovascular events. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards models, adjusting for demographics, lifestyle, and dietary confounders. Secondary analyses will examine changes in intermediate biomarkers (blood pressure, HbA1c, lipids).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, longitudinal evidence on how processing modifies the health effects of nuts. It will refine public health messaging and dietary guidelines by moving beyond 'nuts are healthy' to specific, actionable recommendations about nut preparation. The findings will also inform food policy and industry practices regarding product formulation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Mozaffarian and others has established nuts as beneficial, no major study has systematically disaggregated the effects by commercially relevant preparation styles (salted, sugar-coated) to challenge the uniform 'healthy' assumption. This directly tests the interaction between a healthy food matrix and added dietary risks (salt, sugar).

proposals.title: Independent Association of Nut Consumption with Blood Pressure: Assessing Confounding by Overall Dietary Pattern in a Prospective Cohort Analysis
proposals.motivation: Observational studies consistently link higher nut intake to lower blood pressure (BP) and reduced cardiovascular risk. However, nut consumers often follow healthier overall diets, making it difficult to isolate the specific effect of nuts. Disentangling this is crucial for evidence-based dietary guidance and understanding whether nuts confer benefits beyond being a marker of a healthy diet.
proposals.hypothesis: The inverse association between habitual nut consumption and systolic/diastolic blood pressure is independent of overall dietary pattern, suggesting a direct or additive benefit of nuts.
proposals.method: We will analyze data from a large, well-characterized prospective cohort (e.g., Framingham Offspring, NHS/HPFS) with validated food frequency questionnaires and repeated BP measures. Using multivariable linear and logistic regression, we will model BP outcomes against nut intake (servings/week), adjusting first for standard covariates (age, sex, BMI, activity). We will then introduce a dietary pattern score (e.g., a Mediterranean Diet Score or a Healthy Eating Index-2015) as a covariate to test for attenuation of the nut-BP association. Sensitivity analyses will include stratification by diet quality and assessment of effect modification.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide stronger causal inference regarding the specific role of nuts in BP regulation. It will clarify whether public health messages should promote nuts as a standalone intervention or primarily within the context of a healthy dietary pattern. The findings will directly inform dietary guidelines for hypertension prevention.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies adjust for individual nutrients or foods, this analysis explicitly tests confounding by a comprehensive, validated measure of overall diet quality. It moves beyond asking *if* nuts are associated with BP to investigating *why*.

proposals.title: Associations of Portion Size and Caloric Intake from Nuts with Cardiovascular Risk: A Re-analysis of Prospective Cohort Data
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies on nuts and cardiovascular disease (CVD) have predominantly used consumption frequency as the primary exposure metric. While this has established a clear inverse association, it fails to capture the dose-response relationship based on actual quantity consumed, which is critical for precise dietary guidance. Understanding whether benefits are driven by consistent small portions or larger, less frequent intakes is essential for translating population-level findings into actionable public health and clinical recommendations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, after accounting for frequency, the average portion size and total caloric contribution from nuts will show a stronger, graded inverse association with incident CVD events than frequency alone. We further hypothesize that the benefits will plateau at higher caloric intakes, defining an optimal range.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pooled analysis of individual-level data from large, pre-existing prospective cohorts (e.g., NHS, HPFS, MESA) with validated food frequency questionnaires (FFQs) that capture both frequency and portion size for nut consumption. The primary exposure will be calculated as average daily grams and kilocalories from nuts. Using Cox proportional hazards models, we will assess associations with incident CVD (myocardial infarction, stroke, CVD death), adjusting for standard confounders and total energy intake. We will compare model fit (AIC) and hazard ratios for frequency-only versus portion/caloric-based models and test for non-linear relationships using restricted cubic splines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed, quantitative evidence defining the relationship between the actual amount of nuts consumed and CVD risk, moving beyond simple frequency. The findings will directly inform more precise dietary guidelines and nutritional counseling by identifying a target intake range. It will also establish a methodological framework for re-evaluating other dietary components where portion size may be a critical, overlooked metric.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard analytical paradigm in nutritional epidemiology by prioritizing quantitative intake over categorical frequency for a specific food. It directly tests the assumption that 'frequency is the primary relevant metric' for nuts, using existing cohort data in a novel way to extract more precise public health guidance.

proposals.title: Biomarker-Calibrated Food Frequency Questionnaires: Validating Self-Reported Intake of Key Dietary Components for Cardiovascular Disease Risk Assessment
proposals.motivation: Food Frequency Questionnaires (FFQs) are the cornerstone of large-scale nutritional epidemiology but are subject to substantial measurement error from recall bias and misreporting. This error obscures true diet-disease relationships, potentially leading to null findings or incorrect effect estimates for critical nutrients like polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs). Pre-2026, there is a pressing need to move beyond the assumption of accurate self-report to strengthen causal inference in dietary research.
proposals.hypothesis: Calibrating self-reported FFQ data against objective biomarkers of intake (e.g., plasma phospholipid PUFAs, urinary selenium) will significantly improve the accuracy of dietary exposure assessment and reveal stronger, more precise associations with incident cardiovascular disease events.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing, pre-2026 prospective cohort studies with stored biospecimens, such as the Cardiovascular Health Study or the Framingham Offspring Study. Using a regression calibration approach, we will model the relationship between self-reported intake of specific foods/nutrients (e.g., fish, nuts, whole grains) and their corresponding biomarkers in a validation sub-cohort. These calibration equations will then be applied to the entire cohort to generate biomarker-calibrated intake estimates. These corrected exposures will be used in Cox proportional hazards models to assess associations with hard CVD endpoints, comparing the hazard ratios and confidence intervals to those derived from standard FFQ data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical method to correct for systematic measurement error in nutritional epidemiology. It will generate more reliable effect estimates for specific dietary components in relation to CVD, directly informing dietary guidelines. The project will also produce a framework for prioritizing which nutrients and foods most require biomarker calibration in future studies.
proposals.novelty: While biomarker substudies exist, this proposal systematically applies calibration to correct population-level FFQ data for key dietary factors linked to CVD, moving from simply identifying error to actively correcting it in etiological analyses. This integrates Mozaffarian's expertise in dietary biomarkers and CVD outcomes with advanced measurement error correction methods.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Machine Learning to Identify Nut-Consuming Dietary Patterns and Their Association with Blood Pressure in a Large Iranian Cohort
proposals.motivation: While nut consumption is linked to lower blood pressure (BP) and cardiovascular risk, individuals do not eat nuts in isolation. Pre-2026, dietary patterns are often defined a priori (e.g., Mediterranean diet), potentially missing complex, data-driven patterns of nut consumption within broader diets. A machine learning approach can objectively identify these real-world dietary clusters, offering a more nuanced understanding of how nuts are consumed in combination with other foods and their true impact on BP.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that unsupervised machine learning will reveal distinct, data-driven dietary clusters characterized by varying levels and types of nut consumption, and that membership in specific nut-rich clusters will be associated with more favorable blood pressure profiles compared to clusters with low or no nut intake, independent of total energy intake and other confounders.
proposals.method: Using dietary data from the Isfahan Healthy Heart Program, we will apply k-means clustering and Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) to food frequency items to identify dietary patterns. Feature importance will be analyzed to characterize clusters by nut intake (walnuts, almonds, pistachios) and other co-consumed foods. We will then use multilevel linear regression, adjusting for demographics and lifestyle, to test associations between cluster membership and systolic/diastolic BP. Model stability will be assessed via bootstrapping.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond simple nut quantity analysis to define holistic, nut-centric dietary patterns in an Iranian population. It will provide evidence for whether the cardiovascular benefits of nuts are maximized within specific dietary contexts, informing more precise, culturally relevant dietary guidelines and targeted public health messaging.
proposals.novelty: This applies unsupervised machine learning, specifically pattern recognition algorithms, to discover nut-related dietary clusters from cohort data, a novel approach compared to pre-2026 methods that typically use predefined scores or factor analysis. It uniquely focuses on how nuts are embedded in overall eating patterns rather than treating them as an isolated exposure.

proposals.title: Ecological Momentary Assessment of Nut Consumption: A Novel Method to Quantify Intake and Investigate Associations with Acute Blood Pressure Responses in a U.S. Cohort
proposals.motivation: Accurate dietary assessment is critical for understanding diet-disease relationships, but traditional methods like food frequency questionnaires are prone to recall bias and misreporting. Nut consumption, linked to cardiovascular benefits in Dr. Mozaffarian's prior work, is particularly challenging to measure due to its episodic and often unplanned nature. Reducing this measurement error is essential for refining risk estimates and understanding true biological effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that smartphone-based ecological momentary assessment (EMA) will capture higher and more frequent nut consumption compared to a 24-hour recall baseline, and that this more accurate real-time data will reveal a stronger inverse association between nut intake and momentary blood pressure measurements.
proposals.method: We will recruit 300 adults at elevated cardiovascular risk. Participants will use a custom smartphone app for 14 days, receiving 4 random prompts daily to report recent nut consumption (type, amount, context) via a structured survey. Concurrently, they will use a validated Bluetooth-connected blood pressure cuff. The app will employ time- and location-stamping, push notifications, and photo-based portion estimation. Data will be analyzed using multilevel mixed-effects models to examine within-person and between-person associations between nut intake (as a time-varying covariate) and systolic/diastolic blood pressure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-frequency, real-world data on nut consumption patterns and their acute physiological correlates. It will validate EMA as a superior tool for measuring episodic foods, directly addressing a key methodological limitation in nutritional epidemiology. The findings will generate more precise effect estimates to inform dietary guidelines for cardiovascular prevention.
proposals.novelty: While EMA has been used for mood and behavior, its application to capture specific, episodic food items like nuts in free-living settings, paired with concurrent physiological monitoring, is novel. This moves beyond assessing long-term diet-disease associations to investigating acute, mechanistic pathways.

proposals.title: Prevalence, Determinants, and Cardiovascular Health Implications of Nut Avoidance in U.S. Adults: A Nationally Representative Analysis
proposals.motivation: Despite strong pre-2026 evidence linking regular nut consumption to reduced cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk, population-level intake remains low. Understanding the specific, modifiable barriers to consumption—beyond simple dietary recall—is critical for designing effective public health and policy interventions to close this evidence-practice gap and improve cardiovascular health at a population level.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, identifiable factors—including perceived cost, allergy/medical avoidance, taste preferences, and lack of awareness of health benefits—are the primary drivers of low nut consumption in the U.S., and that these barriers are disproportionately prevalent among populations with higher CVD risk.
proposals.method: We will analyze cross-sectional data from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES, 2017-2020 cycles). The primary outcome is self-reported nut avoidance or low consumption (<1 serving/week). We will use multivariable logistic regression models to identify independent demographic, socioeconomic, and health belief determinants of avoidance. A secondary analysis will link avoidance status to measured CVD risk factors (blood pressure, lipid profiles) and calculated 10-year ASCVD risk scores, adjusting for confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first nationally representative quantification of the reasons for nut avoidance in the U.S. adult population. It will directly inform targeted nutritional counseling and public health messaging by identifying the most prevalent barriers. Furthermore, it will estimate the potential population-level CVD risk burden attributable to these modifiable behavioral factors.
proposals.novelty: While prior research establishes the benefits of nut consumption, this study shifts focus to the causes of non-consumption, applying a behavioral and access-based lens to a well-established dietary CVD prevention strategy. It moves beyond ecological associations to model individual-level barriers and their potential health impact.

proposals.title: Health and Economic Trade-offs of Trans-Fat Replacement: A Comparative Modeling Study of Palm Oil versus Unsaturated Vegetable Oils in the US Food Supply
proposals.motivation: The global removal of industrial trans-fatty acids (TFAs) from the food supply is a major public health achievement. However, the choice of replacement fat has significant implications. Pre-2026 evidence indicates palm oil, a common replacement, raises LDL cholesterol, while unsaturated oils (e.g., soybean, canola) lower it. A direct, policy-relevant comparison of the long-term cardiometabolic disease burden and associated economic costs of these replacement strategies is urgently needed to guide industry and regulatory decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing TFAs with unsaturated vegetable oils will be more cost-effective than replacement with palm oil, leading to greater reductions in incident cardiovascular disease and diabetes, and lower long-term healthcare costs, despite potential differences in oil price and stability.
proposals.method: We will adapt a validated US-based microsimulation model (e.g., CVD PREDICT) to compare three scenarios: current TFA intake, replacement with palm oil, and replacement with a mix of unsaturated oils (high-PUFA). Inputs will include pre-2026 meta-analyses on TFA, saturated fat (palmitic acid), and PUFA effects on blood lipids and incident disease. We will use nationally representative dietary intake data (NHANES) to model population-level changes in fat consumption. Cost-effectiveness analysis will incorporate oil price data, disease treatment costs, and quality-adjusted life-years (QALYs), using a healthcare sector perspective over a 10-year and lifetime horizon.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative comparison of the health and economic consequences of the two primary TFA replacement pathways. It will deliver actionable estimates of preventable cardiovascular events, diabetes cases, and net healthcare savings, directly informing food industry formulation decisions and public health policy. The findings will extend the evidence on dietary fats beyond single-nutrient effects to practical, population-level trade-offs.
proposals.novelty: While the harms of TFAs and benefits of unsaturated fats are well-established, the specific, modeled comparison of the dominant commercial replacement (palm oil) against optimal nutritional replacements (unsaturated oils) in a cost-effectiveness framework is novel and addresses a critical real-world policy gap identified post-TFA bans.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Burden of Industrially-Produced Trans-Fatty Acids on Stroke, Type 2 Diabetes, and All-Cause Mortality: A Comparative Risk Assessment Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: While the causal link between trans-fatty acid (TFA) intake and coronary heart disease (CHD) is well-established, the global disease burden attributable to TFA beyond CHD remains less quantified. Pre-2026 evidence suggests TFA consumption also increases risk for ischemic stroke and type 2 diabetes (T2D), major drivers of global morbidity and mortality. A comprehensive, comparative risk assessment is urgently needed to inform national and international food policy, as many regions still lack effective TFA elimination strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of global stroke, T2D, and all-cause mortality is attributable to current levels of industrially-produced TFA consumption, and that modeled elimination of these fats would yield substantially larger health gains than estimates based on CHD alone.
proposals.method: We will employ a comparative risk assessment framework, integrating dose-response relationships from meta-analyses of prospective cohort studies (e.g., RR per 2% energy from TFA for stroke and T2D) with global dietary intake data from pre-2026 sources like the Global Dietary Database. Disease-specific mortality and disability-adjusted life year (DALY) data will be sourced from the Global Burden of Disease Study. We will model the population attributable fraction and avertable burden for stroke, T2D, and all-cause mortality across 185+ countries, comparing scenarios of current intake versus theoretical minimum risk exposure level (<0.5% of total energy).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, country-specific estimates of the global burden of stroke, T2D, and mortality attributable to TFA, extending beyond prior CHD-focused models. It will deliver a powerful, evidence-based tool for policymakers to advocate for swift implementation of WHO's REPLACE action package and similar regulations by demonstrating the full scope of preventable disease.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond the established CHD-TFA paradigm to synthesize and quantify the multi-disease impact of TFA, integrating emerging pre-2026 epidemiological evidence on stroke and T2D into a unified global burden model to reveal the total health cost of inaction.

proposals.title: Modeling the Evolving Cardiovascular Health Impact of Trans-Fatty Acid Policies: A Dynamic Simulation of Intake Trends and Replacement Fat Patterns (2000-2025)
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, policies to reduce industrial trans-fatty acid (TFA) intake have been implemented with varying speed and scope globally. Static models fail to capture the dynamic shifts in population diet, including the lag in policy adoption and the critical, heterogeneous nature of the fats (e.g., saturated vs. unsaturated) that replace TFAs in the food supply. This limits accurate assessment of the past and future cardiovascular disease (CVD) burden attributable to these policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating time-varying data on TFA intake and replacement fat patterns will reveal a more nuanced and temporally accurate estimate of the cardiovascular health benefits (and potential risks) of TFA reduction policies than static models, with outcomes significantly influenced by the specific macronutrient substitutions.
proposals.method: We will extend established comparative risk assessment frameworks (e.g., the US IMPACT model) by integrating serial, nationally representative dietary data (e.g., NHANES, FAO supply data) to create annual estimates of TFA intake and replacement fats (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated) from 2000-2025. Using a microsimulation or discrete-time Markov cohort model, we will apply time-specific hazard ratios (from meta-analyses linking fats to CVD events) to a simulated US adult population. The model will compare a 'policy-off' scenario (constant pre-policy intake) against the dynamic 'policy-on' scenario to estimate attributable CVD events and deaths.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first temporally granular, policy-relevant analysis of the US TFA reduction effort, quantifying the health impact year-by-year.
